common_adulterants: Argemone oil | Mineral oil | Kesari dal oil
notes: Primary test in North India. Argemone oil is most dangerous adulterant — causes epidemic dropsy.

common_adulterants: Sunflower oil | Soybean oil | Pomace oil
notes: Extra virgin shows higher wavelength absorption. Adulteration with seed oils very common in India.
Solid below 24°C. If liquid at room temp it may be adulterated with liquid oils.
Very light absorption. Any colour shift indicates dye or cheap oil mixing.
High smoke point. Peanut allergies make adulteration detection critical.

common_adulterants: Palm kernel oil | Vanaspati | Mineral oil
notes: Naturally orange-red due to carotenoids. Bleached palm oil is a common adulterant base.

common_adulterants: Rice bran oil | Groundnut oil | Mineral oil
notes: Bauer-Villavecchia test specific to sesame. Strong aroma if pure.
High oryzanol — unique biomarker. Used to detect adulteration via chromatography.

common_adulterants: Palm oil | Mineral oil | Corn oil
notes: Bland taste. Extremely common base for blended oil adulteration.
Very low erucic acid — adulteration with high-erucic oils is a concern.
Golden yellow. Adulteration increases β-carotene levels — detectable by light sensor.
Contains gossypol — toxic if unrefined. Adulteration with heavier oils detectable by density.
Highest linoleic acid of all oils — adulteration shifts fatty acid profile.
Highly prone to oxidation. Dark amber — very high absorption. Keep refrigerated.
Pale golden. Premium oil — frequently adulterated with cheaper kernel oils.
Very high density — easiest to identify pure by viscosity. Used in industrial applications.
Deep green due to chlorophyll. Colour fade indicates adulteration or refining.
Rich omega-3 — adulteration with cheaper oils reduces nutritional value significantly.
Light greenish — extracted from wine production byproduct. Mild flavour.
Widest acceptable range due to blend variability. Adulteration with non-edible oils is highest risk.
